commit.message:
[analysis] Modal participation and M2 theory/empirical gap resolution

Resolves the 3.75x discrepancy between the SDOF model prediction
(~7165 um) and ISO 2631 empirical data (~1911 um) for abdominal
displacement under 0.5 m/s^2 whole-body vibration.

Four correction factors quantified:
- Modal participation Gamma_2 = 0.48 (partially-constrained oblate
  spheroid breaks parity symmetry that decouples base excitation
  from even-order modes)
- Geometric nonlinearity: x0.73 (Donnell-von Karman + amplitude-
  dependent damping, already established)
- Transmission path: ~1.00 (spine amplification offset by soft-
  tissue absorption — approximately neutral at n=2 frequency)
- BC radiation damping: x0.74 (skeletal attachments increase
  effective zeta from 0.125 to 0.169)

Combined factor: 0.263, yielding corrected prediction of 1884 um
(within 1.4% of ISO 2631 datum).

New files:
- src/analytical/modal_participation.py: participation factors,
  multi-mode FRF, path model, gap budget, figure generation,
  LaTeX paragraph for paper discussion
- data/figures/fig_transmissibility_comparison.png: four-panel
  comparison figure (SDOF vs multi-mode vs ISO)

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

Model the urinary bladder as a fluid-filled viscoelastic shell using
AbdominalModelV2 with bladder-specific parameters (fill-dependent

- n=2 flexural mode: 12-18 Hz across fill volumes (50-500 mL)
- Minimum f2 ~ 12 Hz near 150-200 mL fill
- Mechanical (WBV) coupling ~7600x more effective than airborne acoustic
- Consistent with ISO 2631 pelvic resonance and occupational health data

commit.message:
Add viscous correction analysis for enclosed fluid damping

Quantify the effect of fluid viscosity on the Q factor of the
abdominal cavity model. The baseline treats the fluid as inviscid;
this analysis adds the Stokes boundary-layer dissipation for the
n=2 flexural mode.

Key results:
- Stokes layer delta/R < 0.02 for all physiological fluids
- Viscous damping adds < 2% to structural damping (mu <= 0.1 Pa-s)
- Q drops from 4.000 to 3.926 in worst case (mucus)
- Crossover viscosity where fluid = structural damping: 281 Pa-s
- Non-Newtonian (power-law) chyme does not alter the conclusion
- Frequency shift < 10 mHz (negligible)

The inviscid approximation is well justified.

New files:
- src/analytical/viscous_correction.py  (analysis module)
- data/figures/fig_viscous_correction.png  (Q vs viscosity figure)
- docs/research-logs/viscous-correction-results.md  (write-up)

commit.message:
[analysis] Solid organ inclusions: homogeneous fluid approximation justified

Hashin-Shtrikman effective medium theory + Eshelby perturbation analysis
shows organ inclusions (liver, spleen, kidneys at phi~0.25) shift flexural
mode frequencies by <1%, vs >100% uncertainty from wall properties.

Key findings:
- Bulk modulus unchanged (tissue K ~ water K, delta < 1 ppm)
- Macroscopic shear modulus = 0 (inclusions don't percolate)
- Only effect is density: rho_eff = 1029 vs 1020 kg/m3 (delta_f ~ -0.4%)
- Liver asymmetry splits m-degeneracy by ~0.9% (observable but irrelevant)
- Inclusion stiffness E has NO effect (all curves collapse in parametric sweep)

Hypothesis confirmed: organs ride along as added mass for flexural modes.

feat: detailed constrained-bubble model for gas-pocket transduction

Extends the preliminary Minnaert analysis with:
- Constrained-bubble model (elastic intestinal wall, hoop-stress stiffness)
- Cylindrical geometry (radial breathing + axial piston modes)
- Multi-pocket collective response (coherent monopole superposition)
- Tissue-coupling via monopole near-field (1/r^2 displacement decay)
- Parameter study: volume 1-100 mL, spherical vs cylindrical, free vs
  elastic vs rigid wall, 1-50 Hz
- Population Monte Carlo (N=10000): log-normal gas distribution, Dirichlet
  pocket partitioning, SPL threshold variability

Key findings:
- Gas pockets give ~1 um displacement at 120 dB (7-10x > cavity resonance)
- Cylindrical pockets are geometry-limited (~1.05 um, set by lumen radius)
- SPL thresholds range 111-120 dB depending on largest pocket size
- Total volume displacement varies 16x across physiological gas range
- At 120 dB, 100% of simulated population exceeds PIEZO threshold
- Mechanism is sub-resonant (no frequency tuning needed)

commit.message:
Add Rayleigh-Ritz analysis for oblate spheroidal shell vibrations

Implements a 2-DOF Rayleigh-Ritz method for flexural vibrations of a
fluid-filled oblate spheroidal shell, comparing with the equivalent-sphere
approximation in natural_frequency_v2.py.

Key features:
- Love-Kirchhoff thin shell theory (membrane + bending + prestress)
  with numerical integration over the oblate surface geometry
- Fluid added mass via interior Laplace solution using oblate
  spheroidal harmonics P_n(i*xi)
- Optimal tangential displacement via variational principle
- Comparison table: modes n=2,3,4 at aspect ratios c/a = 0.5-0.9

Results show the sphere model overestimates flexural frequencies by
~10-20% at the default geometry (c/a = 0.67), with errors growing
for more oblate shapes.

commit.message:
Pin ClangSharp as local tool to prevent version mismatch (#2236)

- Add .config/dotnet-tools.json pinning ClangSharpPInvokeGenerator to
  17.0.1 so builds use the correct version regardless of what is
  installed globally (a newer global version like v18 generates
  NativeAnnotation attributes that the emitter does not handle,
  causing GetDeclarationDiagnostics to take ~68 minutes instead of
  ~28 seconds)
- Generate a local tool manifest at build time in the scratch directory
  so this works both in-repo and when the GeneratorSdk is consumed as
  a NuGet MSBuild SDK package by external projects
- Invoke ClangSharp directly via its DLL path instead of dotnet tool run
  to avoid per-invocation overhead across 963 partition items
- Fix thread-safety bug: replace non-atomic ret &= ProcessPartition()
  in Parallel.ForEach with Interlocked.Increment on a failure counter
- Increase default scraping parallelism from ProcessorCount/2 to
  ProcessorCount
- Remove GenerateNuGetConfig and global tool install path
